explain.rule: Non-defining relative clauses add EXTRA information about a noun — the information is not essential to identify which one. They are separated by COMMAS. Use who (people), which (things), where (places), when (times), whose (possession). CANNOT use 'that' in non-defining clauses. If you remove the clause, the sentence still makes complete sense. Compare: defining (no commas, essential info) vs non-defining (commas, extra info).
explain.why_matters: Non-defining clauses are common in formal writing, journalism, and sophisticated speech. They let you add extra information smoothly without starting a new sentence.
explain.hebrew_note: Hebrew uses ש with relative clauses regardless of type. In English the distinction between defining (no commas, that/who/which) and non-defining (commas, who/which — NO that) is a B2 skill that marks formal educated English.
explain.tip: Comma test: if the clause is in commas and you could remove it without losing the main point → non-defining. No commas and the sentence needs the clause to make sense → defining. Also: non-defining = which (never that), who (never that for people). Written English only — in speech the pause indicates it.

good_examples.sentence: My sister, who lives in London, is visiting next week.
good_examples.audio_text: My sister, who lives in London, is visiting next week.
good_examples.note: Extra info about my sister — I only have one sister

good_examples.sentence: The Eiffel Tower, which was built in 1889, attracts millions of tourists.
good_examples.audio_text: The Eiffel Tower, which was built in 1889, attracts millions of tourists.
good_examples.note: Extra fact about the Eiffel Tower — we already know which tower

good_examples.sentence: Tel Aviv, where I grew up, has changed enormously.
good_examples.audio_text: Tel Aviv, where I grew up, has changed enormously.

good_examples.sentence: My manager, whose opinion I respect greatly, agreed with my proposal.
good_examples.audio_text: My manager, whose opinion I respect greatly, agreed with my proposal.
good_examples.note: Possession — extra info about the manager

good_examples.sentence: The meeting, which lasted three hours, was exhausting.
good_examples.audio_text: The meeting, which lasted three hours, was exhausting.

good_examples.sentence: She moved to Berlin in 2015, when she started her PhD.
good_examples.audio_text: She moved to Berlin in 2015, when she started her PhD.

good_examples.sentence: My car, which I bought two years ago, is already having problems.
good_examples.audio_text: My car, which I bought two years ago, is already having problems.
good_examples.note: Extra info — we know which car (my car)

good_examples.sentence: Professor Cohen, who teaches economics, has just won an award.
good_examples.audio_text: Professor Cohen, who teaches economics, has just won an award.
good_examples.note: We already know who Professor Cohen is

good_examples.sentence: The company's CEO, whose name I can't remember, resigned yesterday.
good_examples.audio_text: The company's CEO, whose name I can't remember, resigned yesterday.

good_examples.sentence: We visited Athens, where the Olympic Games originally began.
good_examples.audio_text: We visited Athens, where the Olympic Games originally began.

bad_examples.wrong: My sister that lives in London is visiting.
bad_examples.correct: My sister, who lives in London, is visiting.
bad_examples.reason: Non-defining clauses cannot use 'that'. Use 'who' for people + add commas.

bad_examples.wrong: The Eiffel Tower, that was built in 1889, is beautiful.
bad_examples.correct: The Eiffel Tower, which was built in 1889, is beautiful.
bad_examples.reason: Non-defining clauses use 'which' for things, never 'that'.

bad_examples.wrong: My car which I bought last year has problems.
bad_examples.correct: My car, which I bought last year, has problems.
bad_examples.reason: Non-defining clauses need commas — both before and after.

bad_examples.wrong: She moved to Berlin in 2015, that was when she started her PhD.
bad_examples.correct: She moved to Berlin in 2015, when she started her PhD.
bad_examples.reason: 'That' cannot be used in non-defining clauses. Use 'when' for time.

bad_examples.wrong: Professor Cohen, which teaches economics, won an award.
bad_examples.correct: Professor Cohen, who teaches economics, won an award.
bad_examples.reason: 'which' is for things, 'who' is for people — even in non-defining clauses.

exercises.question: My brother, ___ is a doctor, works in a hospital in Haifa.
exercises.hint: Person in non-defining clause → who (not that)

exercises.question: The Colosseum, ___ was built in 70 AD, is still standing.
exercises.hint: Thing in non-defining clause → which (not that)

exercises.question: We visited Kyoto, ___ we spent most of our time.
My boss, ___ car is always the newest model, loves technology.
The company was founded in 1952, ___ was just after the war.
exercises.hint: Referring to the whole situation → which
exercises.explanation: which — referring back to the whole clause.

exercises.question: She won her first championship in 2018, ___ she was only 19.
My parents, ___ both retired now, travel a lot.
exercises.hint: People in non-defining clause — include the verb too

exercises.question: This book, ___ I read years ago, is now a film.
My sister, ___ works as a nurse, has just had a baby.
The new library, ___ was designed by a famous architect, opens next month.
exercises.explanation: which — thing, non-defining. That is not used in non-defining clauses.

exercises.question: Which sentence is correct?
exercises.answer: The president, who gave a speech yesterday, flies to Paris today.
exercises.distractors: The president that gave a speech yesterday flies to Paris. / The president, that gave a speech yesterday, flies to Paris today. / The president who gave a speech yesterday flies to Paris.

exercises.question: Which is a non-defining relative clause?
exercises.answer: The Taj Mahal, which is in India, was built in the 17th century.
exercises.distractors: The book that I bought is interesting. / I know a woman who speaks six languages. / She lives in the house that was built in 1900.

exercises.question: They moved to Barcelona in 2010, ___ they met for the first time.
My laptop, ___ I've had for three years, is starting to slow down.
Find and fix: 'My sister that lives in London is visiting.'
exercises.answer: My sister, who lives in London, is visiting.
exercises.hint: Non-defining needs commas + who not that

exercises.question: Find and fix: 'The Eiffel Tower, that was built in 1889, is beautiful.'
exercises.answer: The Eiffel Tower, which was built in 1889, is beautiful.

exercises.question: Find and fix: 'My car which I bought last year has problems.'
exercises.answer: My car, which I bought last year, has problems.
exercises.explanation: commas before and after non-defining clause.

exercises.question: Find and fix: 'Professor Smith, which teaches physics, won an award.'
exercises.answer: Professor Smith, who teaches physics, won an award.
exercises.hint: which is for things, who is for people
exercises.explanation: who — Professor Smith is a person.

exercises.question: Put the words in the correct order:
exercises.answer: My brother, who is a pilot, travels constantly.
exercises.explanation: My brother, who is a pilot, — commas around the extra info.

exercises.question: Put the words in the correct order:
exercises.answer: The meeting, which lasted three hours, was exhausting.
exercises.explanation: which lasted three hours — non-defining, commas.

exercises.question: Put the words in the correct order:
exercises.answer: We moved in 2019, when we started the project.

speak.sentence: My colleague, who has been with the company for twenty years, is retiring next month.
chunks: My colleague, who has been with the company for twenty years,

speak.sentence: The hotel, which we stayed in last summer, has been completely refurbished.
chunks: The hotel, which we stayed in last summer,

speak.sentence: My father, whose advice I always follow, told me to take the job.
chunks: My father, whose advice I always follow,
told me to take the job.

speak.sentence: She graduated in 2015, when she was just twenty-two years old.
chunks: when she was just twenty-two years old.

speak.sentence: The city centre, where most of the shops are located, is always very busy.
chunks: The city centre, where most of the shops are located,

speak.sentence: The prime minister, who gave a press conference this morning, denied the allegations.
chunks: The prime minister, who gave a press conference this morning,

speak.sentence: My laptop, which I only bought last year, has already stopped working.
chunks: My laptop, which I only bought last year,

speak.sentence: We visited Athens, where the ancient Olympic Games originally took place.
chunks: where the ancient Olympic Games originally took place.
